query: diagnostic accuracy of AP vs lateral X-ray for detecting spondylolisthesis

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbar Spondylolysis and Spondylolisthesis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of patients with low-back pain typically includes anterior-posterior (AP) and lateral radiographs of the impacted area. Some physicians will obtain radiographs of the entire spine. A key component to radiographic evaluation is obtaining flexion/extension films, which illustrates vertebral segment stability. [18] Serial standing lateral radiographs obtained in the clinic will help track any potential progression of the slip.

retrieved_docs.title: Spondylolisthesis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Anteroposterior, lateral, and flexion-extension radiographs are first-line diagnostic tools, allowing assessment of vertebral alignment, dynamic instability, and grading using the Meyerding classification (see Images . Meyerding's Grading and Lumbar Spine Lateral X-Ray). Meyerding's classification of spondylolisthesis is based on the percentage of anterior translation of the slipped vertebra relative to the adjacent vertebra. Grade I spondylolisthesis is 1% to 25% slippage, grade II is 26% to 50% slippage, grade III is 51% to 75% slippage, and grade IV is 76% to 100% slippage (see Image . Grade II Spondylolisthesis). Grade V spondylolisthesis or spondyloptosis is >100% slippage.

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbosacral Spondylolisthesis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Following the history and examination, the best screening tool is an AP and lateral weight-bearing X-ray of the lumbar spine. [15]  Lumbosacral spondylolisthesis can be best assessed mainly on the lateral view, but occasional coronal deformity should not be missed. In cases where a clinical examination indicates an abnormal sagittal balance of the spinal column, a whole-spine lateral standing X-ray is indicated. In the majority of cases, an isthmic defect is detected on radiographs; however, in cases of doubt, an MRI scan is recommended for further evaluation. Oblique X-rays of the lumbosacral junction, Computerized Tomography (CT) scans, and SPECT scans may also help identify the defect; however, they involve exposure to ionizing radiation.

retrieved_docs.title: Spondylolisthesis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging plays a critical role in differentiating these conditions. While plain radiographs are often adequate for detecting significant vertebral displacement, advanced imaging such as MRI or CT is necessary to identify subtle pars defects, stress reactions, or other pathologies. For instance, MRI may be preferred in early-stage stress reactions due to its sensitivity to edema, whereas CT is superior in visualizing bony abnormalities. Failure to recognize mimicking conditions risks mismanagement and potential progression of underlying pathology. Finally, the possibility of 2 or more concurrent pathologies is not rare. For example, the concurrent presence of spondylolisthesis in a level and stenosis in an adjacent level.

retrieved_docs.title: Traumatic Lumbar Spondylolisthesis(Archived) -- Evaluation -- Radiologic Investigations
retrieved_docs.content: A commonly adopted method of grading spondylolisthesis is the Meyerding classification which is based on the percentage of the distance the anteriorly translated vertebral body has moved forward relative to the superior endplate of the body. Grades using this system are as follows: Grade I: 0% to 25% (or low grade) Grade II: 26% to 50% Grade III: 51% to 75% Grade IV: 76% to 100% Grade V: greater than 100% (also known as spondylosis)

retrieved_docs.title: Spondylolisthesis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of spondylolisthesis encompasses various conditions that present with similar symptoms, eg, low back pain and radiculopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Spondylolisthesis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography (CT) is the gold standard for identifying pars defects and bony abnormalities, particularly with sagittal reconstructions. It is essential for preoperative planning, such as in evaluating the size of the pedicle for the selection of the pedicle screw size. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is preferred for assessing neural elements, disc degeneration, and soft tissue pathology. MRI is particularly sensitive for detecting early stress reactions and bone marrow edema, though it may have limitations in identifying chronic pars defects. The degenerative spondylolisthesis is usually associated with central stenosis, while the isthmic spondylolisthesis typically is not. The reason is that the intact pars interarticularis in degenerative spondylolisthesis leads to a ventral slippage of the inferior articular processes and the lamina. In isthmic spondylolisthesis, the pars defects disconnect the inferior articular processes and the lamina, which are consequently not being displaced. Single photon emission CT can help detect early stress reactions or pars defects when findings on radiographs and CT are inconclusive.

retrieved_docs.title: Sciatica -- Differential Diagnosis -- Spondylolisthesis
retrieved_docs.content: Spondylolisthesis is commonly classified as one of 5 major etiologies: degenerative, isthmic, traumatic, dysplastic, or pathologic. Forward translation of the vertebrae may cause a spinal canal narrowing at the slip's level. In severe L5/S1 slips, the L5 nerve root is most commonly affected by central and lateral recess stenosis, whereas foraminal stenosis results in L4 nerve root compression. The anteroposterior and lateral plain films and lateral flexion-extension plain films are the standard for the initial diagnosis of spondylolisthesis. [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Spondylolisthesis -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care
retrieved_docs.content: Radiological evaluation in the immediate postoperative period is obtained to confirm the absence of hardware complications and to function as a baseline study for future comparisons. Standing radiographs or CT scans are commonly performed at discharge and then x-rays during follow-up visits. The exact time intervals for imaging depend on surgeon preferences and the level of concern for potential complications. Bracing, eg, lumbar-sacral orthoses, may be used selectively based on the surgeon’s preference and the patient’s stability needs.

retrieved_docs.title: Isthmic Spondylolisthesis(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential Diagnosis: Degenerative spondylolisthesis Traumatic spondylolisthesis Dysplasatic spondylolisthesis Pathologic spondylolisthesis (i.e., neoplasm, infection, iatrogenic)

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbosacral Spondylolisthesis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: MRI scans are more sensitive in identifying pars lesions.MRI can also identify stress reactions that occur even before a fracture line develops. [16] In dysplastic cases, a dome-shaped or significantly inclined sacrum can present, as well as trapezoid-shaped L5 and dysplastic facets of S1. Neoplasms and infections are an extremely rare primary cause of spondylolisthesis, but should merit consideration as a differential diagnosis in patients with constitutional symptoms. To assess dynamic instability, flexion and extension views should be obtained. Either 4 mm of translation or 10 degrees of angulation of motion compared to the adjacent motion segment is diagnostic for spondylolisthesis. Grading of the forward slip is classified by the Meyerding classification: Grade I <25% of the width of the vertebra on the lateral view Grade II 25 to 50% Grade III 50 to 75% Grade IV 75 to 100% Grade V >100% (spondyloptosis) Pelvic incidence (PI) has a direct correlation to Meyerding's grade. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbosacral Spondylolisthesis(Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Despite the estimate of up to 6% of the population suffering from spondylolisthesis, the majority of them are asymptomatic. Only a small percentage of symptomatic cases will require surgical treatment. The majority of the worst prognoses occur in cases of: Very young age at presentation Female Slip angle >45 degrees High-grade slip Degenerative slip (most commonly in adults)

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbar Spondylolysis and Spondylolisthesis(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: One of the crucial measurements to note regarding slips is the amount of translation between vertebral bodies. The amount of “uncovering” of one endplate from the other determines the grade. There are four grades of spondylolisthesis: Grade 1: 0% to 25%, Grade 2: 26% to 50%, Grade 3: 51% to 75% and Grade 4: 76% to 99%. [12] [19] At 100% displacement, patients have developed spondyloptosis. [20] Grades 1 and 2 are considered low-grade slips. [21] Grades 3 and 4 are considered high-grade spondylolisthesis. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Spondylolisthesis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Degenerative lumbar disc disease is a common mimic of spondylolisthesis, encompassing entities, eg, discogenic pain syndrome, facet syndrome, and lumbosacral spondylosis. These conditions can present with mechanical low back pain or radiculopathy but typically lack evidence of vertebral displacement on imaging. Similarly, lumbar disc herniation frequently causes radicular symptoms and sensory deficits but is differentiated by the absence of vertebral slippage and by imaging findings of disc extrusion or protrusion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 983
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as “اعتلال مرتكز العظم” functions as a parent category that includes spondylosis, intervertebral disc disorders, and other back problems. These specific spinal and disc pathologies are therefore understood as manifestations of the broader bone‑centered disease entity, allowing clinicians to classify and address them within a unified diagnostic and therapeutic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Transmission imaging is linked to صورة مقطعية محوسبة, indicating that this imaging modality encompasses computed tomography scans. In clinical practice, صورة مقطعية محوسبة frequently co‑occurs with صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية, reflecting that CT imaging is often performed together with spinal‑region myelographic or spinal cord imaging to provide complementary anatomical detail for diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: Spondylopathies/spondyloarthropathy (including infective) is a classification that encompasses spondylosis, intervertebral disc disorders, and other back problems, grouping these diverse spinal conditions under a common diagnostic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1899
retrieved_docs.content: MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES is a broad classification that encompasses a variety of diagnostic and therapeutic interventions, and within this category the specific procedure صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية is listed as a subordinate (child) procedure, indicating that spinal‑region imaging is considered one of the miscellaneous diagnostic and therapeutic techniques.

retrieved_docs.content: Spondylosis, intervertebral disc disorders, and other back problems are all considered subcategories of اضطراب العمود الفقري (spinal disorder), indicating that these specific back conditions fall under the broader classification of spinal disorder and should be approached within that overarching diagnostic and therapeutic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 480
retrieved_docs.content: tomography imaging, and shared anatomical targets. This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding the placement of each imaging modality within the larger diagnostic radiology framework, facilitating appropriate selection and interpretation of studies for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2602
retrieved_docs.content: MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES is a broad procedural category that includes various imaging modalities. Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan is classified as a child of this category, indicating its role as a diagnostic imaging technique used for cross‑sectional visualization of internal structures. The procedure described in Arabic as صورة نخاعية للمنطقة الشوكية (spinal cord imaging of the spinal region) is also listed as a child of MISCELLANEOUS DIAGNOSTIC AND THERAPEUTIC PROCEDURES, highlighting its inclusion as another specialized diagnostic imaging method. Together, these relationships illustrate that both CT scanning and spinal cord imaging are organized under the same overarching procedural group, emphasizing their shared purpose in providing diagnostic information for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 291
retrieved_docs.content: Tomografie is recognized as a medical diagnostic imaging modality; it is classified within the broader category of plain X‑ray techniques and is considered a specific type of medical diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal and unspecified findings in the cerebrospinal fluid and lumbar puncture (LP) are both clinically associated with headache, indicating that the presence of headache may reflect either atypical cerebrospinal fluid results or be a common post‑procedural symptom following an LP.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1421

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2111
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical vertebra fracture (كسر في الفقرة الرقبية) is classified within the broader category of other fractures, and it frequently co‑occurs with pelvic fracture (كسر الحوض). Recognizing that a cervical vertebra fracture belongs to the group of other fractures and that it often appears alongside a pelvic fracture can guide clinicians to assess for additional injuries when either injury is identified, thereby improving comprehensive patient evaluation and management.
